cue: How do you say someone loves their food?

es.meaning: To have a hearty appetite; to be a big eater.
es.example: Tu hijo tiene buen diente, repitió tres veces.
es.exampleTranslation: Your son's a good eater — he went back three times.
es.regionNote: Also 'ser de buen comer'. Reviewer to confirm MX currency.

it.meaning: To love eating; to have a big appetite.
it.example: Tuo figlio è una buona forchetta, ha fatto il bis tre volte.
it.exampleTranslation: Your son loves his food — three helpings.

bridge.note: A compliment paid with two different objects: Spanish credits the TOOTH — the body doing the work — while Italian credits the FORK, the tool. And the grammar follows the metaphor: in Spanish you HAVE a good tooth (something you possess), in Italian you ARE a good fork (something you become at the table). Have versus be, body versus instrument, in six words.

interference.es_to_it: 'Avere una buona forchetta' is the classic slip — Italian says ESSERE una buona forchetta. You are the fork.
interference.it_to_es: 'Ser un buen diente' misfires; Spanish keeps it possessive: TENER buen diente.

review.notes: Discovered via an Instagram card set Joe shared 2026-07-20 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh). ES counterpart chosen by Claude from recall — UNVERIFIED. Third have/be entry — a themed run with 0080 and the acqua alla gola pair is now possible.
